text:
I initiate a timestamping routine with my phone at the dining table. I stand facing the table holding my phone, say “we need a stopwatch,” swipe and start using it, then ask “Mark a timestamp, right?” while everyone looks on. I place the phone in front of each person, instruct each to mark the timestamp, pass the phone to Katrina who passes it to Alice and Tasha, retrieve the phone after everyone marks it, turn it off, tell someone “Turn it on,” and then put the phone away. Conclusion: a timestamp was recorded by each participant and the device was secured for the meeting.

I move to the small tripod and open the morning meeting. I walk up to and hold the small tripod, state that we’ll “just discuss a bit” and that it’s a morning meeting about what to do on the last day, and introduce the items on the dining table by putting my hand on a hard drive and asking “What’s this called?” I describe that the morning should focus on discussion and propose installing the tripod as a backup if discussion stalls; I take a quick photo of the tripod. Shure interjects to suggest opening items first to see what they are. Conclusion: I set meeting objectives and proposed the tripod as a backup action while prompting inspection of items.

I coordinate and observe the dismantling of hard drives and assign expertise roles. I move to the left and watch everyone take apart the hard drives on the table; when Lucia asks “Only 4 of them?” I confirm “Yes, only 4.” Lucia notes she’s taken one apart before and I acknowledge her experience, then point at Shure and refer to him as the mentor/tutor. Shure offers that anyone needing a power bank can take one from the supplies. Conclusion: dismantling proceeds with role recognition and resource guidance provided.

I collect and relocate boxes and cables to organize materials. I pick up a box, say “I’ll take these away first,” organize data cables in my hand, walk backward and forward to return a data cable to the box, close and re-open the box as needed, move one box onto another, pick up two boxes, crouch to place a box on a shelf, then return to the dining table to oversee packing. Conclusion: boxes and cables were gathered and relocated to storage as part of tidying and inventory.

I sort disposables and designate items to keep or discard. Holding a black plastic bag and the small tripod, I ask what can be made from dismantled parts, move the tripod aside, tell the group to “keep this box” and instruct them to throw away the box contents. I remove the box liner, hold a plastic bag and a piece of paper, consolidate items into a new plastic bag, respond to Shure’s note to keep the lens cloth, retrieve Alice’s cardboard box and the liner, then crouch and place the trash into a box while saying “let’s arrange it like this for now.” Conclusion: trash was consolidated, keep/discard decisions were enacted, and materials were staged.

I set up the microphone as a facilitation tool and orient everyone to the presentation area. I return to the dining table, steady the microphone and say we’ll use it “when everyone is deep in thought,” offer to explain first, hold the microphone in my right hand, walk left while pointing to the screen on the left and say “So, you can see this place.” Conclusion: the microphone was positioned for facilitated discussion and I directed attention to the presentation screen.

I invited everyone into my bedroom/workstation and guided them to the desk. I told them they couldn't see the place, said "Or you guys can come over" and "Walk around," waved to beckon, pointed at the screen and bedroom, stepped back to watch Shure stand up and walk over, then walked into the room myself; I pushed the stool forward, steadied it, pointed at my desk and said "No, no, please come in" while Shure asked, "Are we trespassing into your private space?" Conclusion: I actively directed the group into my private workstation area, positioned the stool for them, and prompted them to approach the desk.

I introduced the workstation layout and identified the computers. I turned and pointed to the room and each computer while saying "Let me introduce my workspace to everyone," counted devices aloud (mentioning seventh, eighth, ninth, tenth while emphasizing "mainly, six computers here"), introduced the computers one by one with their names written, pointed at the first computer and said "For example, this is my L1," stated "All your data is in here," and listened to Shure reply "This is our second life" while watching everyone laugh. Conclusion: I mapped and labeled the computers, emphasized that each corresponds to one of the glasses, and identified where the data for the group is stored.

I explained the data transfer process and the hard drive/cable setup. I stated that after each recording session, every three hours the data will transfer to a hard drive, pointed at the hard drive and computer while saying "You'll plug these glasses into this thing" and "Now always connecting to this first hard drive," demonstrated that the hard drive has two cables (one for connecting to the computer and one for power), pulled out the stool, gestured to show the power source makes the hard drive "pretend it's on a computer" to simulate a computer environment so the hard drive will operate, fiddled with the cables and closed my laptop to show how it looks. Conclusion: I presented the periodic transfer schedule and demonstrated the roles of the hard drive and its two cables (data and power) to enable simulated-computer operation for data ingest.

We discussed contingency for sudden power loss and considered backup power. Shure asked what happens in a sudden power outage; I responded "That's hard to say," noted that if the computer suddenly loses power "it would be awkward," and the exchange trailed off; Shure proposed "Like a USB backup power supply? Like those backup power sources," while I continued walking and touched my face, and Shure asked another question. Conclusion: the vulnerability to power outage was acknowledged, and Shure suggested backup power options, but no definitive mitigation was provided in that discussion.

I told the team to begin installing after confirming resources. I checked Shure, walked forward while holding a small tripod, and told the group “We’re not that rich yet,” “We can only rely on this villa,” and “Or trust Beijing Electric.” I set the small tripod in front, said “Alright, you guys start installing,” added “You can chat while installing” and suggested they could bring something first while people began talking. This established the decision to start the installation and delegated the installation task to the others.

I repositioned the small tripod and checked whether the glasses would obstruct the setup. I moved the tripod left, pointed at Alice and raised both hands saying “These glasses,” walked behind Alice and said “They shouldn’t cover,” then asked “They shouldn’t cover, right?” while looking at Tasha and Alice and stating “You’re not covering anything now.” This clarified that the glasses would not interfere with the installation.

Lucia instructed on aligning and inserting a component while I steadied parts and followed her guidance. Lucia identified a hole and where the part needed to sit, demonstrated “align it like this” and “then slowly push it in,” warned it was fragile and to be gentle; I held and steadied the small tripod, stepped back and watched Shure take a step and bend over as he assisted, and Lucia cautioned “don’t tip it over” and to apply a little force. This recorded the alignment and cautious insertion procedure under Lucia’s direction with Shure assisting.

I unpacked and prepared the box components and handed screws to Shure for tightening. I picked up the black box with both hands, pulled out the box component, noted “There are a lot of screws,” examined it, reached into the box and handed a screw to Shure; Lucia instructed that screws needed tightening, pointed out a notch and where to pull a strip, and Shure confirmed placement. This documented the transfer of screws and the plan to tighten them as part of assembly.

I moved the stand and stool into position, sat on the stool, and we located all screw holes for assembly. I picked up the stand and placed it to my left, dragged and held the stool forward, sat on the stool holding its top with both hands while Lucia pointed out the long screw hole and an internal screw hole; Alice asked whether there were 4 or 8 bolts, Lucia confirmed there are 8, Alice screwed in one, and I answered Alice’s question about insertion. This completed the identification of the screw locations and the start of bolt installation.

I observed tool characteristics and the group’s progress on fastening. While watching everyone tidy up and work, Lucia noted the screwdriver isn’t magnetic and that magnetic attraction had been a prior issue; Shure and others acknowledged this and continued assembling. This summarized the remaining technical note about tools and the continuation of the assembly process.

The team evaluated whether the larger part would fit and identified a fit constraint. I looked toward the center of the whiteboard while Lucia said, "this one is for the bigger ones" and demonstrated positioning; Katrina confirmed "the big one can fit in," and Lucia noted "it can't really go through this hole" then added "you just have to." I watched everyone fix things and turned my head to observe adjustments. Conclusion: the group established that the larger piece is intended for the big slot but cannot pass through a particular hole, and they proceeded with corrective adjustments.

I addressed Tasha's hair blocking the camera and supervised its correction. I looked at Tasha and told her, "Your hair is always blocking the camera, can you do something about it?", reached out and pointed; Tasha asked "This one?" and I suggested "Yeah, or tuck it inside." Tasha said she had already tucked it in, then fixed her hair and picked up her glasses; Lucia instructed "You just need to fix it in place" and Katrina recommended "Find a small clip." I pulled my hand back, stood up briefly to observe, turned my head from side to side to check, then sat down after confirming "Yeah, this is good" while watching Tasha finish. Conclusion: Tasha adjusted her hair per the group's requests and the camera obstruction was resolved.

The group decided on background music, shared preferences, and had brief local-interest conversation led by Shure. Shure asked for background music and offered to play; Lucia approved. The group mentioned preferences (Katrina: David Tao / R&B; Lucia: Western/random; Alice: "Let's play something energetic"), and we joked about upbeat Northeast China music until music started. I and others laughed and I suggested dancing; Shure demonstrated an embedding/placement ("Just embed this halfway...Like this") and I agreed "Yeah, just fix it." I sang with Shure and asked about his origin—he said Changchun in Jilin Province after I asked follow-ups—and the group acknowledged that. Conclusion: music was played, preferences were exchanged, lighthearted interaction occurred, and Shure's hometown (Changchun) was confirmed.

I led and executed several hands-on fixing steps and performed a final check. I got up and stepped forward, pointed at Tasha's items saying "This goes in the back," held Tasha's box and tightened a screw by hand while saying "Look at both sides," and held a black box with both hands. I walked between Alice and Tasha, proposed "We can just use this strip" (noting "we threw it away last time anyway"), tested the strip ("This strip fits perfectly"), and suggested placement options ("We can put it here"). I picked up something from Shure, said "I successfully put it in yesterday," then picked up a stick and put it into the box, saying "Yes." I downplayed prior concern ("It doesn't matter," "It's just for aesthetics"), pointed at the box and asked "Is it done?", observed "It looks like there are no screws," and clapped. Conclusion: I positioned parts, secured a screw, added a strip/stick for appearance, and performed a visual completion check before signaling the task as done.

I pick up and open the first cardboard box to start the packing task. I turn left, reach out, walk forward and pick up a cardboard box, open it, put my hand inside, pick up a data cable, tidy the data cables and put the cable back; Alice, Katrina and Lucia comment about electrician/soldering experience and internships while Shure says “Ready to go in.” I complete the initial inspection and return the data cable to the box to prepare for the next step.

I display the box and move it along the assembly area while coordinating the next workstation. I pick up the box again, hold it up to show everyone and look around the group; I walk back carrying the box, listen as Lucia mentions the next assembly line connects to the computer and Shure says the next job needs a senior technician, then I close the box. The box is closed and staged while the team identifies skill requirements for the next station.

I handle adjustments and small fastenings at the workstation and test fit parts. I place a box on the bed, walk to a stool, step over it and sit; I stand, reach out to two screws, cup and adjust parts of a Shure box, step back, sit again and state “2,000,” resting my hands on the stool top while watching others. I finish the adjustments and remain positioned to observe further assembly activity.

I monitor others’ assembly work and direct placement into the box. I watch everyone fix things, sway and lean while talking with Shure and Lucia; I instruct “Yes, yes, put it in this box,” point at the box and say “This is the entrance, yes, this is the last step,” then hold out my hand to let Shure speak. The team follows the direction and proceeds to place items into the designated box.

I retrieve the box from Shure and finalize the packing for that task. After Shure places items into the box, I hold it with both hands, take it back (described as driving him eastward and grabbing the box back), close and straighten the box; Shure announces everyone can exit and thanks the group. The packing for that task is completed, everyone claps, and the box is secured.

I continue with box handling and stacking while the group discusses next actions and invitations. I take another cardboard box, stack two boxes, hold them to my chest, move left across the room and place a box on the stool and on the bed at different points; the group discusses who to invite on the last day (Sunday) and suggests writing names on the blackboard, with Shure offering to write. The team agrees to note invitees on the blackboard and boxes are restacked and repositioned for upcoming tasks.

I move toward organizing the workspace and propose additional assembly. I stand up, move the two stacked boxes in front of me, walk forward into the foyer, point at the blackboard and suggest assembling a shelf, pass by Shure and pick up another cardboard box. The workspace is reorganized and a plan to write invitees and consider shelf assembly is initiated.

I moved a box from one spot to another while coordinating with others. I held the box to my chest as I walked forward, turned right and placed it on the left-hand side, rotated it with both hands, told others “you can put these down,” and watched the little hands pack the box; Shure said “Let's assemble it later; let's write first,” and I repeatedly said “It's not a big problem.” Summary: the box was transported, positioned, and packed while I and Shure agreed to postpone assembly and prioritize writing.

I continued handling the box and set it aside. I kept walking forward carrying the box (holding it in my right hand) and put the box in the corner. Recap: the box was secured in the corner and removed from active handling.

I searched for a pen while moving through the dining/bedroom area and asked others for help. I repeatedly asked “Where's the pen?”, turned around and walked up to the dining table, went left, looked toward the table and the bed, Lucia said “Looking for my iPhone?” and laughed, I walked out of the room and continued forward searching for my pencil while Shure walked by; I lowered my head to look and said “The pen should be up there, hold on.” Recap: I actively searched the room for a writing implement and signaled a probable location to Shure.

I moved between floors while carrying a clapperboard and navigated entryways. I turned right to go up the stairs, kept my left hand on the handrail as I continued up, entered (noted “I come in”), then later held the director's clapboard in my left hand, walked down stairs while holding it, turned right and walked down, and during the descent Lucia asked about guests; I kept going down, turned left and entered through a door. Recap: I ascended and then descended stairs while carrying a clapboard and proceeded through an entry door amid a question about guests.

I retrieved and placed the pen on the table after entering. After entering through the door and amid ongoing conversation (Katrina said “Me.”), I picked up the pen and placed it on the table; Shure made a remark (“Me too, I'm a …”). Recap: the pen was located and set on the table for use.

I arranged seating and took a seat while initiating a question about type labels. I placed the stool, held it with both hands, sat down with my hands crossed and looked at Shure, asked “What's the difference?”, turned left to look at Shure and right to look at everyone as the group discussed personality labels (Lucia, Katrina, Shure, etc.). Recap: I positioned myself on the stool and prompted clarification while observing the group discussion.

I observed and participated minimally while Shure led a writing-and-invite planning session on the board. Shure walked up to the board and wrote (“The final day's list” and other notes); I watched Shure write, leaned back and forth, laughed, spoke about how characters/strokes are written, and followed remarks from Alice and Tasha; Shure asked how many people to invite and proposed starting with one; during the planning I nodded, extended and withdrew my hand, pointed at Shure when a tick-mark method was suggested, and continued watching as Katrina suggested drawing little people and Tasha decided to invite someone (a girl); everyone addressed Shure while I maintained observation and occasional verbal confirmations. Recap: Shure led the board work and guest-invitation planning, while I observed, confirmed small decisions, and followed the group's choices.

I watched Shure and the group as they sketched on the whiteboard and made rapid verbal comments about the drawings. I tilted my head, rocked and swayed while observing, laughed repeatedly with others, and voiced brief remarks about the drawings (e.g., "This braid," "This connection isn't on top of the head," "Wig," "Abstract," "Three legs," "Ultraman," "Loving children," "I should invite a boy"). Shure apologized for accidentally drawing the head; I kept watching and laughing as everyone continued writing. 

I prompted how to label the drawings and actively gestured to influence placement of labels. I leaned forward, reached out my right hand and withdrew it a few times while saying "You can write a name under everyone" and "Write their host" (clarifying "like your code name or something"), then suggested alternatives ("Just write one under this little guy" or "write one above or on the face or something"). Shure responded to my prompts by writing on the board while the group watched.

I monitored and confirmed ownership of individual figures as the group assigned names and codes. Shure asked whose figure belonged to whom; Alice identified one as Tasha's and Katrina suggested writing "LYX." Shure said he could record it or write an "L" and continued to write while I nodded, smiled, and kept watching.

I followed the group's discussion about the guest list and gender and affirmed the planned guest count. Shure counted and reported there were six guests; I confirmed "4-6 guests, OK," put my hand on the board briefly, nodded, and continued to observe as Alice commented "All girls" and Shure asked about gender and invitations.

I proposed recruitment sources and agreed to incentive ideas while physically fidgeting and addressing the group. I spoke about inviting "people from around here" and suggested sourcing via Xiaohongshu or "grabbing people directly at Universal Studios," tapped and patted the stool while speaking, and responded affirmatively when Lucia and Tasha discussed offering eggs as an incentive ("If they come, we can give them eggs"). The group accepted these outreach and incentive options as part of the invitation plan.

Shure opens the meeting by announcing immediate next steps ("Tomorrow, we'll get reported here"), and I watch and react while the group settles into planning. I sway my body forward and back, laugh with everyone, and continue watching Shure as he begins writing; others also comment and watch. The meeting begins with Shure presenting the plan and the group attending to what he writes.

Shure documents the plan on the board/backboard while the group observes and comments on its form. Shure repeatedly writes and draws ("This is our plan," "Suspicious plan," "Step aside," "Riddle"), Lucia notes many question marks and likens it to a riddle, and I remain intently watching Shure write, swaying and turning my head as he works. Shure leads the drafting on the board and the team watches his progressing outline.

The team discusses adding an "invite the neighbors" element and the difficulty of defining that character. Shure says "Second, invite the neighbors" and remarks the "invite" character is hard to write; Tasha proposes bringing eggs as an incentive ("If you come, I'll give you this basket"), Alice comments on a vintage feel, and I sit, lean back, and say I will explain and keep reading what Shure writes. The neighbors/invite item is raised explicitly as part of the plan and noted for further clarification.

The group assesses personnel needs and reaches a working headcount. I state "The crew, we still have 5 people," prompt an overall review ("Then let's see overall" / "Finally, how many people do we have to host?"), and Shure counts aloud ("one, two, three, four... four plus five, nine") concluding "Nine to eleven people"; I confirm, say "Yeah, let's start with nine to eleven," nod, and gesture while watching him write. The team therefore frames staffing around a nine-to-eleven person range.

Technical and sequencing concerns about production and post-production are raised and noted. Lucia asks if the team can handle something technically; Shure replies it's "a hassle in post-production" and questions "How much trouble for post-production?"; I move toward and steady the stool, point at the whiteboard, pull back my hand, and continue observing Shure as he enumerates points ("First point OK," "the personnel is confirmed," "three sub-points about personnel," "then we have the second point"). The team acknowledges post-production effort and proceeds to detail personnel and subsequent plan points on the board.

Throughout the session I remain physically engaged and mediate simple actions while Shure finalizes the written plan. I stand up, move forward, steady the stool, sit back down, turn my head repeatedly, lean back holding the stool, and laugh with everyone; I also say I will explain the plan to everyone and keep reading what Shure writes while he occasionally walks to the side. I stay actively involved in observing, confirming counts, and preparing to relay the documented plan to the group.

Shure opened the meeting and began writing on the whiteboard while I observed and nodded. Shure said "To start" and wrote at the board; I stayed seated at first, leaned forward to watch him write, and nodded in response to his prompts. This established the session start and my role as an attentive participant observing Shure’s facilitation.

Shure prompted discussion about the activity content and the questionnaire; participants confirmed they had filled it out. Shure asked whether the questionnaire content was generated by GPT; I laughed, Katrina confirmed she wrote hers (not GPT), and Shure made additional verbal prompts at the board. The group confirmed questionnaire completion and moved forward with the activity discussion.

Katrina presented a flower-based activity plan emphasizing a seven-day cultivation cycle and craft outcomes. Katrina proposed giving each person a vase, letting flowers bloom over 1–2 days within a seven-day cycle, using petals for pressed flowers to make postcards, embedding petals in acrylic glass she bought, and producing related peripherals; she said the items could be distributed on the activity day. I held and fiddled with a box during her presentation, watched Shure at the whiteboard, and gestured on the table as Katrina described materials and methods. The plan defined the activity purpose (flower cultivation and handmade crafts) and the intended execution (pressing petals, acrylic embedding, and distribution).

I handled the delivered flowers and positioned them for the activity. I carried a cardboard box (flowers) to the dining table, straightened and placed the box on the table, moved it forward, and took items out as Katrina opened the box; the delivery was noted as shipped by SF Express from Yunnan. I also moved between the foyer, dining room, bedroom, and restaurant areas as the group discussed sourcing and timing for blooms. The box placement and unpacking prepared the materials for the upcoming demonstration.

The group organized a brief showcase and gathered necessary tools before starting hands-on work. I proposed "showcase a bit first" and walked up to Shure’s side; Alice asked where the scissors were and Shure indicated their location, while Katrina noted she had bought many bunches including carnations. I walked around to locate tools, spoke with Shure while moving forward, then sat down to continue observing. The team confirmed tool availability and agreed to a short demonstration before full execution.

Participants opened materials, began informal demonstrations, and then started packing up while I monitored and tidied. Katrina opened the flower box and I took items out; I stood astride a stool and watched others pack up, rock back and forth while observing, and noted Lucy and Alice packing their things as I continued to watch and tidy the area. The unpacking, brief demonstration, and ensuing cleanup moved the session toward closure while I maintained oversight of materials and space.

Shure approached me as I checked my phone and the session wound down with final confirmations about flower types. Shure walked up to me; I looked at him, then looked down, swiped open my phone, opened an application and swiped left within it; Alice asked whether the flowers were peonies, and Katrina confirmed she had bought too many peonies. I reached out and closed the door, completing my final physical and digital actions as the meeting ended.

I close the door with my left hand and then work on the camera: I fiddle with and operate the camera using both hands, hold it with my right while turning and adjusting with my left, put my left hand under it to tighten a screw and continue tightening until secure, look at the camera, pull my hand back and turn around. Summary: I completed door and camera setup actions and ensured the camera's screws were tightened.

Katrina, Tasha, Alice and others discuss the flowers and care requirements while I listen and ask identification questions: Katrina says the flowers will take one to two days to bloom, pressed flowers need two to three days and the blooms need two to three days of care; Tasha mentions nutrients soaked in water speed blooming; Katrina and Alice ask whether the flowers are lilies, freesia or lily of the valley and note there are two colors (one white and one yellow); Katrina says she thinks two packages were sent and that once the flowers slowly bloom we can use the petals or press and flatten the buds. Summary: from the discussion we established bloom and pressing timelines, identified possible flower types and confirmed packages and intended use of petals.

We agree on the activity format and materials and I contribute logistics and craft ideas: Katrina proposes each of the six of us gets one arrangement and says she already bought the vases and that everyone can distribute and care for the flowers; Alice instructs "wash first" and mentions "vase"; I note I bought postcards and greeting cards and propose using acrylic double-layer glass frames as gifts, collecting leaves outdoors to arrange into pictures, making bookmarks so everyone can create one, and distributing finished items on the last day—Katrina says the plan fits a one-to-three day bloom window and that we'll see the bouquet on the dining table in about one to three days. Summary: format decided—six allocations, vases purchased, pressing and craft uses planned (frames, pictures, bookmarks) and an approximate timeline set.

I oversee packing and immediate task assignments and then take the next item: I watch everyone packing up and turn my head to check people, ask whether anyone else ordered packages and whether anyone bought anything (Shure and Lucia reply "Not yet"), tell people to chat first and that we'll do the next one later, ask "Who's next?", then volunteer "I'll handle this one," put my right leg on the stool, move the box on the left further left, reach forward and find a bunch of veggie leaves. Summary: I confirmed package status, delegated that others continue chatting, and took responsibility for the next box (containing veggie leaves) to handle.

Shure calls on Lucia to present and I watch as she takes her turn. Shure says, “Next is Lucia,” Lucia replies, “Okay, I’ll go,” and sits; I look around, continue watching everyone, pull my right leg back, tilt my head, and keep my gaze on the group and the whiteboard while Lucia prepares to speak. The turn moves to Lucia presenting her ideas and I remain observant.

Lucia proposes an Earth Day–themed DIY project (jellyfish lamps) and I follow her explanation. Lucia says her idea relates to DIY for Earth Day and that she wanted to make something eco-friendly; she explains a jellyfish lamp method—using a bubble tea cup lid as the main part and adding ribbons or beads—and mentions she saw a tutorial online and that these lamps have been popular at scenic spots; I nod slightly, lean forward, and at one point lay face down on the dining table rocking back and forth while continuing to watch. The proposal for the jellyfish lamp is presented as a concrete DIY method using simple materials.

Lucia discusses materials, kits, and potential alternatives while I continue to observe. She notes the lamp originally comes with a materials kit you can buy online, wonders whether there’s a hot glue gun available, and suggests either finding alternatives or buying one; she then proposes that everyone could each make one of the small lights together, and I nod in agreement and keep watching the conversation. The group is offered both a buy-and-assemble option (materials kit) and an on-site DIY option with alternatives to a hot glue gun.

Lucia offers a second DIY idea (cardboard dog door decoration) and practical construction options; I track the details. Lucia describes a low-carbon, recyclable cardboard dog: for a simple version, just cut cardboard, draw a dog head, and stick it on; for a more complex version, cut more pieces and assemble a larger model (she mentions having seen a Doberman tutorial); I nod repeatedly, rock slightly, and rest my hands on the stool while watching. The cardboard dog idea is presented with stepwise methods for simple versus complex builds and examples of achievable variations.

Shure and Lucia confirm material availability and fit with the theme while I move briefly and rejoin the group. Shure comments that they have plenty of cardboard and says the cardboard option is very doable; Lucia adds that it’s environmentally friendly and fits the Earth Day theme; during this exchange I stand up, turn to the right, walk toward the kitchen, then sit back on the stool gripping it with both hands, sway slightly, and say “Yeah.” The group verifies feasibility and theme alignment for the cardboard option while I physically reposition and acknowledge the plan.

The team shifts to logistics for filming and I respond to timing constraints. Shure states, “We are shooting some short films today,” and Lucia notes they are short on time and that writing a script now might not finish in time; she suggests using ready-made scripts or short videos like those seen before and says if there’s a ready-made script they can try to shoot it; I place my hands on my cheeks, then put them down, say “Okay,” nod, scan the group, and continue watching the whiteboard. The group frames a practical filming approach: prefer ready-made or pre-existing short scripts due to time limits.

The meeting closes with name banter and prop preparation while I observe and react. Participants joke about names and a written name (Alice: “You wrote his name backwards,” Katrina and Shure exchange comments about symmetry and “mutation”), I lean back, nod slightly, smile and laugh while looking at the whiteboard, speak briefly, and watch Katrina sit down; finally Tasha says, “OK, I’ll get my props out.” The session ends with light banter confirmed and Tasha taking responsibility to gather props.

I entered the room, moved the stool, and opened a box to inspect its contents. I stood up, walked forward and stepped over the box, pulled the stool back, leaned over and opened the box repeatedly while rummaging and taking out multiple sheets of paper; I held a sheet with both hands as Shure asked why his props were in my suitcase and I answered briefly. In short, I inspected the box contents and retained several papers while others questioned the origin of the props.

I handed one of the papers to Tasha and repositioned myself near the dining table as the group discussed unpacking and alternatives. I stepped over the box, handed the paper to Tasha, walked to the right and then stood in front of the dining table looking at Tasha; Tasha asked whether to unpack, suggested she would go get a ready-made one, and instructed someone to take out a petri dish while Shure confirmed “petri dish.” In summary, I transferred the paper to Tasha and joined the table discussion focused on unpacking and the petri dish.

I checked my phone to verify deliveries and product details while continuing to watch the group. I turned on my phone, opened apps (WeChat and a map), scrolled and typed, said aloud that carnations and “peony carnations” had been bought and that the vase had arrived yesterday (delivered by SF Express), and Katrina noted which packages belonged to the group; I then instructed everyone to turn off their phones and place them on the dining table. To recap, I used my phone to confirm purchases and deliveries and asked others to stop using their phones so the group could proceed.

Tasha explained the seed-paper item and suggested using a petri dish for sprouting; I observed and asked about unpacking. Tasha described that she had designed some seed paper made from discarded coffee grounds, processed into pulp that contains seeds (she specified sunflower seeds as easier to sprout) and mentioned using a petri dish for hydroponics; Katrina and Shure added practical notes about placing flowers, leaves, or something sandwiched in the middle and a fragrance frame, while I watched everyone pack up their things. In short, Tasha provided the purpose and method of the seed paper and recommended the petri dish method, and I monitored the group’s next steps.

I checked the situation about a rejected item and considered next actions. I took a paper with my right hand, reported “It got rejected,” asked “Should I unpack it?” and nodded to Tasha as I watched others handle notes and pack up. In conclusion, I identified the rejection, queried whether to unpack the item, and awaited or coordinated the group’s decision.

I presented my seed-paper designs to the group while holding pieces of paper and watching Tasha place a sheet on the table: I stated they can sprout in about five days, explained I made different designs—five kinds in total, including two simple, earth-related ones—and confirmed when Katrina asked that they were my original designs; I repeatedly identified them as "seed paper," said they are more environmentally friendly and aesthetically pleasing, and emphasized they are fairly sturdy.
